items.title: How the sequential pattern of pituitary hormone loss helps stage hypopituitarism
items.content:
When a pituitary or hypothalamic process progressively compresses or destroys the gland, trophic hormone loss does NOT occur randomly or all at once — it follows a fairly predictable ORDER:
GH > FSH/LH > TSH > ACTH (GH is lost earliest/most often; ACTH deficiency tends to occur latest).
• Clinically, this means a patient with documented deficiency of one hormone likely has diminished RESERVE of hormones "downstream" in this sequence, even if their baseline levels still look normal.
• The presenting feature differs by age: in CHILDREN, growth retardation (reflecting GH loss) is often the first clue; in ADULTS, hypogonadism (reflecting gonadotropin loss) is typically the earliest symptom.
This ordered vulnerability shapes the practical work-up: once any deficiency is found, the other axes — especially those later in the sequence — should be screened, and re-evaluated over time as compressive lesions evolve.
keyPoints: Order of hormone loss with progressive pituitary damage: GH > FSH/LH > TSH > ACTH.
A documented single-hormone deficiency raises suspicion for diminished reserve of 'downstream' hormones.
Growth retardation is the earliest clue in children; hypogonadism is the earliest symptom in adults.
items.reference: TABLE 391-1 Etiology of Hypopituitarism: "Trophic hormone failure associated with pituitary compression or destruction usually occurs sequentially: growth hormone > follicle-stimulating hormone > luteinizing hormone > thyroid-stimulating hormone > adrenocorticotropic hormone. During childhood, growth retardation is often the presenting feature, and in adults, hypogonadism is the earliest symptom."

items.title: Why diagnosing hypopituitarism requires pairing a low trophic hormone with a low target-organ hormone
items.content:
A trophic (pituitary) hormone level in isolation is hard to interpret — a "normal" TSH or LH/FSH can actually be INAPPROPRIATELY normal if the target gland hormone is already low.
• Secondary HYPOTHYROIDISM: diagnosed by a LOW free T4 together with a LOW or inappropriately NORMAL TSH — a high TSH would instead point to primary thyroid failure.
• Secondary HYPOGONADISM: diagnosed by a LOW testosterone (or estradiol) WITHOUT the expected compensatory RISE in gonadotropins — elevated gonadotropins would instead indicate primary gonadal failure.
The core logic: in pituitary disease, the trophic hormone fails to RISE in response to falling target-hormone levels — the absence of an appropriate feedback response is itself the diagnostic clue, not an isolated trophic hormone value.
keyPoints: Pituitary hormone deficiency = trophic hormone that is low/inappropriately normal WHILE the target-organ hormone is also low.
Secondary hypothyroidism: low free T4 + low/normal TSH (not high TSH).
Secondary hypogonadism: low testosterone/estradiol WITHOUT elevated gonadotropins.
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION: "Biochemical diagnosis of pituitary insufficiency is made by demonstrating low levels of respective pituitary trophic hormones in the setting of low levels of target organ hormones. For example, low free thyroxine in the setting of a low or inappropriately normal TSH level suggests secondary hypothyroidism. Similarly, a low testosterone level without elevation of gonadotropins suggests hypogonadotropic hypogonadism."

items.title: How PROP1 and Pit-1 mutations produce distinct patterns of combined pituitary hormone deficiency
items.content:
Pituitary cell lineages develop under the control of a cascade of transcription factors. Two illustrate how a single gene defect produces a PREDICTABLE combination of hormone deficits:
• Pit-1 mutations → combined GH + PRL + TSH deficiency. Patients present with growth failure and variable hypothyroidism; the pituitary may look hypoplastic on MRI.
• Prop-1 acts UPSTREAM of and is required for Pit-1 function. PROP1 mutations therefore cause a BROADER combined deficiency: GH + PRL + TSH + GONADOTROPINS. Over 80% have growth retardation; by adulthood ALL are TSH- and gonadotropin-deficient, and a minority later add ACTH deficiency. Paradoxically, the pituitary can appear ENLARGED on MRI in some cases (rather than hypoplastic).
The clinical lesson: the specific COMBINATION of deficient hormones (and even pituitary size on imaging) can point toward the underlying transcription-factor defect.
keyPoints: Pit-1 mutation → GH + PRL + TSH deficiency, often with growth failure and hypothyroidism.
PROP1 acts upstream of Pit-1 → broader deficiency (GH + PRL + TSH + gonadotropins), with ACTH deficiency emerging later in a minority.
PROP1 mutations can paradoxically cause an ENLARGED pituitary on MRI, unlike the hypoplastic gland typical of isolated Pit-1 defects.
items.reference: DEVELOPMENTAL CAUSES OF HYPOPITUITARISM: "Pit-1 mutations cause combined growth hormone (GH), prolactin (PRL), and thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) deficiencies... Prop-1 is expressed early in pituitary development and appears to be required for Pit-1 function. Familial and sporadic PROP1 mutations result in combined GH, PRL, TSH, and gonadotropin deficiency. Over 80% of these patients have growth retardation; by adulthood, all are deficient in TSH and gonadotropins, and a small minority later develop adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) deficiency."

items.title: Why Kallmann syndrome causes both anosmia and hypogonadotropic hypogonadism
items.content:
Kallmann syndrome links two seemingly unrelated deficits — sense of smell and reproductive function — because of a shared DEVELOPMENTAL ORIGIN.
• During embryogenesis, GnRH-producing neurons originate in the OLFACTORY PLACODE and must MIGRATE from there into the hypothalamus, traveling alongside developing olfactory bulb axons.
• Mutations (e.g., in the X-linked KAL/ANOS1 gene, and many others since identified) disrupt this migration.
• The SAME developmental defect therefore produces BOTH: failure of olfactory bulb formation (anosmia/hyposmia) AND failure of GnRH neurons to reach the hypothalamus (GnRH deficiency → hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, absent puberty).
Clinically: males present with delayed puberty and micropenis (low testosterone in infancy); females present with primary amenorrhea and absent secondary sexual development — both WITH an impaired sense of smell.
keyPoints: GnRH neurons and the olfactory bulb share a common embryonic origin (olfactory placode) and migratory pathway.
A single migration defect (e.g., KAL/ANOS1 mutation) therefore causes both anosmia/hyposmia AND GnRH deficiency.
Presentation: delayed puberty, micropenis (men) or primary amenorrhea (women), plus impaired smell.
items.reference: HYPOTHALAMIC ENDOCRINE DYSFUNCTION: "Kallmann syndrome results from defective hypothalamic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) synthesis and is associated with anosmia or hyposmia due to olfactory bulb agenesis or hypoplasia... The initial genetic cause was the X-linked KAL gene, mutations of which impair embryonic migration of GnRH neurons from the hypothalamic olfactory placode to the hypothalamus."

items.title: How GnRH stimulation testing helps (and fails to help) localize the cause of hypogonadism
items.content:
Intravenous GnRH (100 µg) is a direct test of PITUITARY GONADOTROPE responsiveness: LH normally rises ~threefold and peaks within 30 minutes; FSH rises less and plateaus over the next 60 minutes.
• A NORMAL gonadotropin RISE in a hypogonadal patient indicates the GONADOTROPES THEMSELVES are intact — the defect must be HYPOTHALAMIC (insufficient endogenous GnRH drive). This is the key diagnostic use of the test, e.g., confirming Kallmann syndrome.
• An ABSENT response, however, is AMBIGUOUS — it can occur with EITHER a pituitary gonadotrope defect OR with prolonged hypothalamic GnRH deficiency (chronically unstimulated gonadotropes become hyporesponsive and may need repeated GnRH exposure to 'prime' them).
• Because of this asymmetry, GnRH testing adds little beyond baseline hormone levels EXCEPT in the specific scenario of suspected isolated GnRH deficiency.
keyPoints: Normal LH/FSH rise after GnRH → intact gonadotropes → points to a hypothalamic cause of hypogonadism.
An absent response does NOT reliably distinguish pituitary from hypothalamic disease (chronic GnRH deficiency itself blunts the response).
GnRH stimulation testing is mainly useful for confirming isolated GnRH deficiency (e.g., Kallmann syndrome).
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (Gonadotropin Deficiency): "In the setting of gonadotropin deficiency, a normal gonadotropin response to GnRH indicates intact pituitary gonadotrope function and suggests a hypothalamic abnormality. An absent response, however, does not reliably distinguish pituitary from hypothalamic causes of hypogonadism. For this reason, GnRH testing usually adds little to the information gained from baseline evaluation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadotrope axis except in cases of isolated GnRH deficiency (e.g., Kallmann syndrome)."

items.title: Bardet-Biedl syndrome: a ciliopathy causing hypogonadotropic hypogonadism with retinal degeneration
items.content:
Bardet-Biedl syndrome is a rare, genetically heterogeneous disorder (linked to at least nine loci, many encoding proteins involved in basal body CILIA function — explaining its diverse multi-organ manifestations).
Key features:
• Intellectual disability, renal abnormalities, obesity.
• Digit anomalies: hexadactyly, brachydactyly, or syndactyly.
• GnRH deficiency in 75% of affected males and half of affected females.
• Central AVP deficiency may or may not be present.
• RETINAL DEGENERATION begins in early childhood — most patients are BLIND by age 30.
The ciliopathy framework explains why a single genetic category produces such a broad, seemingly unrelated phenotype: cilia are required for normal development/signaling across the kidney, retina, and hypothalamic-pituitary axis.
keyPoints: Genetically heterogeneous ciliopathy (≥9 loci) causing intellectual disability, renal disease, obesity, and digit anomalies.
GnRH deficiency in 75% of males and 50% of females; AVP deficiency is variable.
Progressive retinal degeneration leads to blindness by age 30 in most patients.
items.reference: HYPOTHALAMIC ENDOCRINE DYSFUNCTION (Bardet-Biedl Syndrome): "This very rare genetically heterogeneous disorder is characterized by intellectual disability, renal abnormalities, obesity, and hexadactyly, brachydactyly, or syndactyly. Central AVP-D may or may not be associated. GnRH deficiency occurs in 75% of males and half of affected females. Retinal degeneration begins in early childhood, and most patients are blind by age 30."

items.subtopic: Hypothalamic Endocrine Dysfunction — Leptin and Prader-Willi
items.title: How leptin signaling defects and Prader-Willi syndrome cause hypothalamic hypogonadotropic hypogonadism
items.content:
Both disorders converge on the same final pathway — REDUCED hypothalamic GnRH output — but via different mechanisms:
• LEPTIN or LEPTIN RECEPTOR deficiency: leptin (an adipocyte-derived signal of energy stores) normally supports GnRH neuron activity. Its absence causes hyperphagia/obesity AND decreased GnRH production, leading to attenuated pituitary FSH/LH synthesis and release — central hypogonadism.
• PRADER-WILLI SYNDROME: a contiguous gene deletion (paternal 15q, including SNRPN and NECDIN) causing hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, hyperphagia-obesity, hypotonia, intellectual disability, and adult-onset diabetes, plus diminished hypothalamic oxytocin/AVP-producing nuclei. That chronic GnRH administration RESTORES LH/FSH release indicates the defect is hypothalamic (deficient GnRH drive), not an intrinsic pituitary gonadotrope problem.
keyPoints: Leptin/leptin-receptor deficiency → reduced GnRH drive → central hypogonadism plus hyperphagia/obesity.
Prader-Willi syndrome (paternal 15q deletion, SNRPN/NECDIN) also causes hypogonadotropic hypogonadism with hyperphagia-obesity and hypotonia.
Restoration of LH/FSH with chronic GnRH administration in Prader-Willi localizes the defect to the hypothalamus, not the pituitary.
items.reference: HYPOTHALAMIC ENDOCRINE DYSFUNCTION (Leptin and Leptin Receptor Mutations / Prader-Willi Syndrome): "Deficiencies of leptin or its receptor cause a broad spectrum of hypothalamic abnormalities, including hyperphagia, obesity, and central hypogonadism... Deficient GnRH synthesis is suggested by the observation that chronic GnRH treatment restores pituitary LH and FSH release."

items.title: Why a postpartum pituitary mass with hyperprolactinemia should raise suspicion for lymphocytic hypophysitis before surgery
items.content:
Lymphocytic hypophysitis is an AUTOIMMUNE inflammatory infiltration of the pituitary that occurs most often in POSTPARTUM women.
• It presents with HYPERPROLACTINEMIA (mildly elevated PRL) and an MRI showing a prominent pituitary mass that can look just like an ADENOMA.
• An elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate supports an inflammatory rather than neoplastic process.
• Because the imaging appearance overlaps so closely with adenoma, a postpartum woman with a NEW pituitary mass should be evaluated for hypophysitis BEFORE proceeding to surgery — unnecessary resection can be avoided.
• The inflammatory process often RESOLVES after several months of glucocorticoid treatment, with pituitary function recovering to a degree that depends on how much damage occurred — though pituitary failure can also be transient OR permanent.
keyPoints: Most common in postpartum women; presents with mild hyperprolactinemia and an adenoma-mimicking pituitary mass on MRI.
Elevated ESR supports an inflammatory etiology.
Consider hypophysitis BEFORE surgery in this clinical setting — glucocorticoids often induce resolution and may restore pituitary function.
items.reference: ACQUIRED HYPOPITUITARISM (Lymphocytic Hypophysitis): "This occurs most often in postpartum women; it usually presents with hyperprolactinemia and MRI evidence of a prominent pituitary mass that often resembles an adenoma... Because it may be indistinguishable from a pituitary adenoma on MRI, hypophysitis should be considered in a postpartum woman with a newly diagnosed pituitary mass before an unnecessary surgical intervention is undertaken. The inflammatory process often resolves after several months of glucocorticoid treatment, and pituitary function may be restored, depending on the extent of damage."

items.title: How immune checkpoint inhibitors cause hypophysitis
items.content:
Cancer immunotherapy with checkpoint inhibitors removes brakes on T-cell activity — and pituitary cells themselves express one of those brake molecules, CTLA-4, making the gland a direct target.
• CTLA-4 INHIBITORS (e.g., ipilimumab): up to 20% of treated patients develop hypophysitis, with HETEROGENEOUS, variable combinations of thyroid, adrenal, islet, and gonadal axis failure.
• PD-1/PD-L1 INHIBITORS (e.g., pembrolizumab, nivolumab): hypophysitis also occurs but may present in a DELAYED fashion relative to CTLA-4 inhibitor-associated cases.
• A GENETIC susceptibility marker exists: HLA type DQ0602 is found in 39% of patients who develop checkpoint inhibitor-associated hypophysitis.
• Practically, pituitary hormone replacement (± high-dose glucocorticoids for the acute inflammatory phase) can be given SAFELY WITHOUT having to stop the cancer immunotherapy.
keyPoints: CTLA-4 inhibitors (ipilimumab): hypophysitis in up to 20% of patients, with variable multi-axis pituitary failure.
PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors: hypophysitis occurs but may present later.
HLA-DQ0602 is found in 39% of affected patients; immunotherapy can usually continue alongside hormone replacement.
items.reference: ACQUIRED HYPOPITUITARISM (Immunotherapy and Hypophysitis): "Pituitary cells express cytotoxic T lymphocyte antigen-4 (CTLA-4), and up to 20% of patients receiving cancer immunotherapy with CTLA-4 inhibitors (e.g., ipilimumab) may develop hypophysitis with heterogeneously associated thyroid, adrenal, islet, and gonadal failure. Hypophysitis is also reported with PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors (e.g., pembrolizumab and nivolumab) and may show delayed presentation. HLA type DQ0602 is associated with checkpoint inhibitor-associated hypophysitis in 39% of such patients. Pituitary hormone replacement, with or without high-dose glucocorticoids, may be safely tolerated with continued immunotherapy."

items.title: Why pregnancy increases the risk of pituitary apoplexy (Sheehan's syndrome)
items.content:
The pituitary gland normally undergoes HYPERPLASTIC ENLARGEMENT during pregnancy (driven mainly by lactotrope expansion under high estrogen). A larger, more metabolically demanding gland is more vulnerable to its own blood supply being compromised.
• This enlargement INCREASES THE RISK of hemorrhage and infarction — particularly around the time of delivery, when peripartum hypotension/hemorrhage can critically reduce pituitary perfusion.
• The resulting infarction is SHEEHAN'S SYNDROME — postpartum pituitary necrosis, a classic cause of acquired hypopituitarism.
• Apoplexy can also occur spontaneously in a PREEXISTING ADENOMA, or in association with diabetes, hypertension, sickle cell anemia, or acute shock — but the pregnancy-related mechanism is distinctive because it arises in an otherwise NORMAL gland that has simply outgrown its perfusion reserve.
keyPoints: Pregnancy causes physiologic pituitary hyperplasia (mainly lactotrope expansion).
The enlarged gland is more susceptible to hemorrhage/infarction, especially with peripartum hypotension or hemorrhage.
Sheehan's syndrome = postpartum pituitary necrosis, a form of pituitary apoplexy in an otherwise normal gland.
items.reference: ACQUIRED HYPOPITUITARISM (Pituitary Apoplexy): "Pituitary apoplexy may occur spontaneously in a preexisting pituitary adenoma; postpartum (Sheehan's syndrome); or in association with diabetes, hypertension, sickle cell anemia, or acute shock. The hyperplastic enlargement of the pituitary, which occurs normally during pregnancy, increases the risk for hemorrhage and infarction."

items.title: Pituitary apoplexy: an endocrine emergency requiring rapid triage between observation and surgery
items.content:
Pituitary apoplexy is acute hemorrhage/infarction within the sella, presenting with SEVERE HEADACHE (often with meningeal signs), bilateral visual changes, ophthalmoplegia, and — in severe cases — cardiovascular collapse and loss of consciousness. It can cause life-threatening hypoglycemia, hypotension/shock, and intracranial hemorrhage.
Management is TRIAGED by neuro-ophthalmologic severity:
• NO visual loss or impaired consciousness → conservative management with high-dose glucocorticoids (covering the likely acute ACTH deficiency) and close observation.
• SIGNIFICANT or PROGRESSIVE visual loss, cranial nerve palsy, or loss of consciousness → URGENT surgical decompression.
Timing matters: visual recovery after sellar surgery is INVERSELY correlated with the time elapsed since the acute event — i.e., delayed surgery yields worse visual outcomes, which is why severe ophthalmoplegia/visual deficits are an indication for EARLY operation. Hypopituitarism is common after apoplexy regardless of management.
keyPoints: Presents with sudden severe headache, visual changes, ophthalmoplegia, and possible cardiovascular collapse.

Conservative management (high-dose glucocorticoids) if no visual loss/impaired consciousness; urgent surgery if there is significant/progressive visual loss, cranial nerve palsy, or loss of consciousness.
Earlier surgery is associated with better visual recovery — delay worsens outcomes.
items.reference: ACQUIRED HYPOPITUITARISM (Pituitary Apoplexy): "Patients with no evident visual loss or impaired consciousness can be observed and managed conservatively with high-dose glucocorticoids. Those with significant or progressive visual loss, cranial nerve palsy, or loss of consciousness require urgent surgical decompression. Visual recovery after sellar surgery is inversely correlated with the length of time after the acute event."

items.title: Why an empty sella usually preserves pituitary function, yet hypopituitarism can develop insidiously
items.content:
An "empty sella" — a partially or apparently totally CSF-filled sella on MRI — is often an INCIDENTAL finding, sometimes associated with intracranial hypertension.
• Most patients have NORMAL pituitary function, because the THIN RIM of pituitary tissue compressed against the sellar walls remains FUNCTIONAL — a small amount of normally-distributed gland can be enough.
• However, hypopituitarism can still develop INSIDIOUSLY over time, presumably as this rim of tissue is gradually compromised further.
• An empty sella can also be the END-STAGE appearance of a pituitary adenoma that underwent silent infarction/involution, with CSF filling the resulting dural herniation.
• Important pitfall: small, FUNCTIONAL adenomas can arise WITHIN the rim of residual normal tissue and may NOT be visible on MRI — an empty sella does not exclude a hormonally active microadenoma.
keyPoints: Empty sella is often incidental; the thin rim of remaining pituitary tissue is usually fully functional.
Hypopituitarism can still develop insidiously, so periodic monitoring is warranted.
An empty sella can represent silent infarction/involution of a prior adenoma — and small functional adenomas within the residual rim may be MRI-occult.
items.reference: ACQUIRED HYPOPITUITARISM (Empty Sella): "These patients usually have normal pituitary function, implying that the surrounding rim of pituitary tissue is fully functional. Hypopituitarism, however, may develop insidiously... Rarely, small but functional pituitary adenomas may arise within the rim of normal pituitary tissue, and they are not always visible on MRI."

items.title: Why cranial irradiation causes delayed, hypothalamus-mediated hypopituitarism over years
items.content:
Radiation-induced hypopituitarism is NOT an immediate effect of beam destruction of pituitary cells — it is a SLOW, PROGRESSIVE process that mainly reflects HYPOTHALAMIC injury.
• Risk correlates with DOSE and TIME since treatment: up to two-thirds of patients eventually develop hormone insufficiency after a median skull-base dose of 50 Gy (5000 rad).
• The TIMELINE is prolonged — hypopituitarism unfolds over 5-15 YEARS, consistent with gradual hypothalamic (not acute pituitary) damage.
• CHILDREN and ADOLESCENTS are especially susceptible.
• The PATTERN follows the familiar hierarchy: GH deficiency is most common, followed by gonadotropin, then TSH, then ACTH deficiency.
• Practical implication: because deficiency of one axis predicts diminished reserve of others, previously irradiated patients require LIFELONG, periodic anterior pituitary function testing — deficits may not appear until many years after treatment.
keyPoints: Radiation-induced hypopituitarism reflects gradual hypothalamic damage, not acute pituitary cell destruction.
Up to two-thirds of patients develop hormone insufficiency after ~50 Gy to the skull base, evolving over 5-15 years.
Pattern: GH deficiency first, then gonadotropin, TSH, and ACTH — mirroring the general sequential vulnerability.
items.reference: ACQUIRED HYPOPITUITARISM (Cranial Irradiation): "Up to two-thirds of patients ultimately develop hormone insufficiency after a median dose of 50 Gy (5000 rad) directed at the skull base. The development of hypopituitarism occurs over 5–15 years and usually reflects hypothalamic damage rather than primary destruction of pituitary cells. Although the pattern of hormone loss is variable, GH deficiency is most common, followed by gonadotropin, TSH, and ACTH deficiency."

items.title: How GH deficiency in children is diagnosed using provocative stimulation testing
items.content:
GH is secreted in PULSES, so a single random GH measurement cannot reliably separate a child with normal GH secretion from one with true GH deficiency — both can show low values between pulses.
• Diagnosis instead relies on PROVOCATIVE TESTING: exercise, insulin-induced hypoglycemia, or other pharmacologic stimuli, which NORMALLY raise GH to >7 µg/L in children.
• Failure to reach this threshold across appropriate stimuli supports a diagnosis of GH deficiency.
• Age-matched IGF-1 levels are NOT sensitive/specific enough to make the diagnosis on their own, but can help CONFIRM it once provocative testing is abnormal.
• Before testing, adrenal and thyroid hormone replacement should be ENSURED adequate — untreated deficiencies in these axes can blunt GH responses and produce a false-positive impression of GH deficiency.
keyPoints: Random GH levels cannot diagnose GH deficiency because secretion is pulsatile.
Provocative testing (exercise, insulin hypoglycemia, etc.) should raise GH >7 µg/L in normal children.
Adequate thyroid/adrenal replacement must be ensured before testing to avoid false-positive results.
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (Disorders of Growth and Development): "Because GH secretion is pulsatile, GH deficiency is best assessed by examining the response to provocative stimuli, including exercise, insulin-induced hypoglycemia, and other pharmacologic tests that normally increase GH to >7 µg/L in children. Random GH measurements do not distinguish children with normal GH levels from those with true GH deficiency."

items.subtopic: Growth Disorders — GH Deficiency in Children
items.title: Why isolated GH deficiency predisposes children to hypoglycemia
items.content:
GH is a COUNTERREGULATORY hormone — one of its normal roles is to OPPOSE insulin's glucose-lowering action (by promoting lipolysis and reducing peripheral glucose uptake).
• When GH is deficient, this counterregulatory brake is LOST, leaving INSULIN ACTION RELATIVELY UNOPPOSED.
• The result is a tendency toward HYPOGLYCEMIA, particularly during fasting — alongside the other classic features of isolated GH deficiency: short stature, micropenis (in boys, from reduced IGF-1-dependent growth in infancy), increased body fat, and a high-pitched voice.
• Recognizing this hypoglycemia tendency is clinically important: an infant or child presenting with unexplained hypoglycemia plus growth failure should prompt evaluation for GH deficiency (and associated pituitary hormone deficits, given the known familial transcription-factor mutations such as Pit-1/PROP1).
keyPoints: GH normally counters insulin action; its absence leaves insulin's glucose-lowering effect unopposed.
This produces a propensity to hypoglycemia, especially with fasting, in GH-deficient children.
Other features: short stature, micropenis, increased fat, high-pitched voice — at least a third have a familial basis.
items.reference: DISORDERS OF GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT (GH Deficiency in Children): "Isolated GH deficiency is characterized by short stature, micropenis, increased fat, high-pitched voice, and a propensity to hypoglycemia due to relatively unopposed insulin action."

items.title: GHRH receptor mutations: a natural model of somatotrope dependence on GHRH signaling
items.content:
Recessive mutations of the GHRH RECEPTOR produce a striking 'experiment of nature':
• Affected individuals have SEVERE PROPORTIONATE DWARFISM with LOW basal GH that CANNOT be raised by ANY stimulus that normally works through this receptor — exogenous GHRH, GH-releasing peptide, or insulin-induced hypoglycemia (which acts partly via endogenous GHRH release) all FAIL to provoke a GH rise.
• Anterior pituitary HYPOPLASIA is also seen — because GHRH signaling drives not just GH secretion but also SOMATOTROPE CELL PROLIFERATION during development.
This condition demonstrates that the GHRH receptor is essential for BOTH the secretory function AND the proliferative expansion of the somatotrope population — when it is absent, the gland is both small and functionally silent for GH.
keyPoints: GHRH receptor mutations cause severe proportionate dwarfism with unstimulable GH (no response to GHRH, GHRP, or hypoglycemia).
Anterior pituitary hypoplasia results because GHRH signaling drives somatotrope proliferation, not just secretion.
Illustrates the dual (proliferative + secretory) role of GHRH receptor signaling in somatotropes.
items.reference: DISORDERS OF GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT (GHRH Receptor Mutations): "Recessive mutations of the GHRH receptor gene in subjects with severe proportionate dwarfism are associated with low basal GH levels that cannot be stimulated by exogenous GHRH, GH-releasing peptide, or insulin-induced hypoglycemia, as well as anterior pituitary hypoplasia. The syndrome exemplifies the importance of the GHRH receptor for determining somatotrope proliferation and hormonal responsiveness."

items.title: Why GH insensitivity (Laron syndrome) presents with HIGH GH but LOW IGF-1 and low GHBP
items.content:
Laron syndrome results from GH RECEPTOR mutations (homozygous or heterozygous), causing partial or complete GH INSENSITIVITY rather than GH deficiency.
• Because the receptor — not the hormone — is defective, the PITUITARY still secretes GH normally (or even at increased levels, since reduced IGF-1 feedback fails to suppress GH release): GH levels are NORMAL or HIGH.
• But peripheral tissues CANNOT RESPOND to GH, so IGF-1 generation fails → IGF-1 levels are LOW.
• The extracellular domain of the GH receptor circulates as GH-BINDING PROTEIN (GHBP); a defective receptor means LESS GHBP is shed → GHBP is DECREASED.
• This triad — high/normal GH, low IGF-1, low GHBP — is the diagnostic signature, and distinguishes Laron syndrome from true GH deficiency (where GH itself is low).
• TREATMENT bypasses the defective receptor entirely by administering recombinant IGF-1 directly.
keyPoints: Laron syndrome = GH receptor defect → GH insensitivity (not GH deficiency).
Diagnostic triad: normal/high GH, low IGF-1, decreased GHBP.
Treatment bypasses the GH receptor by giving IGF-1 directly.
items.reference: DISORDERS OF GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT (GH Insensitivity): "Homozygous or heterozygous mutations of the GH receptor are associated with partial or complete GH insensitivity and growth failure (Laron syndrome). The diagnosis is based on normal or high GH levels, with decreased circulating GH-binding protein (GHBP), and low IGF-1 levels."

items.title: STAT5B mutations: a cause of GH insensitivity that also produces immunodeficiency
items.content:
STAT5B is the principal intracellular signaling molecule activated by the GH receptor — but it is ALSO used by cytokine receptors important for immune cell development.
• STAT5B mutations therefore cause a COMBINED phenotype: short stature with NORMAL or ELEVATED GH and LOW IGF-1 (GH signaling failure, similar to Laron syndrome) PLUS clinically significant IMMUNODEFICIENCY.
• This combination is a clue that distinguishes STAT5B defects from isolated GH-receptor mutations: a short child with a GH-insensitivity hormone profile WHO ALSO has recurrent infections should raise suspicion for a STAT5B mutation.
• Very rarely, similar GH-insensitive profiles can result from defective IGF-1, IGF-1 receptor, or IGF-1 signaling, or from circulating GH receptor antibodies causing acquired GH insensitivity.
keyPoints: STAT5B is the shared downstream signaling node for both the GH receptor and certain cytokine receptors.
STAT5B mutations cause GH insensitivity (normal/high GH, low IGF-1) PLUS immunodeficiency.
A short-stature child with a Laron-like hormone profile AND recurrent infections suggests STAT5B mutation.
items.reference: DISORDERS OF GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT (GH Insensitivity): "STAT5B mutations result in both immunodeficiency as well as abrogated GH signaling, leading to short stature with normal or elevated GH levels and low IGF-1 levels."

items.subtopic: Growth Disorders — Nutritional and Psychosocial Short Stature
items.title: Distinguishing nutritional and psychosocial short stature from true GH deficiency
items.content:
Not all short stature with a 'GH-insensitive-looking' biochemical profile reflects a primary GH axis defect — ACQUIRED, REVERSIBLE causes can mimic it:
• NUTRITIONAL short stature: caloric deprivation/malnutrition, uncontrolled diabetes, and chronic renal failure all impair GH RECEPTOR function (via proinflammatory cytokines that block GH signal transduction). The biochemical pattern — NORMAL or ELEVATED GH with LOW IGF-1 — can resemble Laron syndrome, but the cause is acquired GH resistance from systemic illness/undernutrition.
• PSYCHOSOCIAL short stature: emotional/social deprivation causes growth retardation WITH delayed speech, paradoxical hyperphagia, and a BLUNTED response to administered GH.
The key distinguishing feature for both: a NURTURING ENVIRONMENT (psychosocial) or correction of the underlying metabolic/nutritional disorder restores growth — unlike fixed genetic causes of GH deficiency/insensitivity.
keyPoints: Malnutrition, uncontrolled diabetes, and chronic renal failure cause acquired GH resistance: normal/high GH with low IGF-1, mimicking Laron syndrome.
Psychosocial deprivation causes growth retardation with delayed speech, hyperphagia, and blunted GH responsiveness.
Both are reversible with correction of the underlying nutritional/psychosocial problem — unlike genetic GH axis defects.
items.reference: DISORDERS OF GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT (Nutritional Short Stature / Psychosocial Short Stature): "Children with these conditions typically exhibit features of acquired short stature with normal or elevated GH and low IGF-1 levels... Emotional and social deprivation lead to growth retardation accompanied by delayed speech, discordant hyperphagia, and an attenuated response to administered GH. A nurturing environment restores growth rates."

items.title: Adult GH deficiency: body composition changes and increased cardiovascular risk
items.content:
Adult GH deficiency (AGHD) usually results from ACQUIRED hypothalamic or pituitary somatotrope damage, following the same sequential vulnerability pattern (GH lost first/most often).
Clinical features cluster around three domains:
• BODY COMPOSITION: reduced lean body mass, increased fat mass with selective INTRAABDOMINAL VISCERAL deposition, and increased waist-to-hip ratio.
• METABOLIC/CARDIOVASCULAR: hyperlipidemia, left ventricular dysfunction, hypertension, increased plasma fibrinogen — and REDUCED BONE MINERAL CONTENT with increased fracture risk.
• QUALITY OF LIFE: social isolation, depression, and difficulty maintaining employment.
Overall, adult hypopituitarism carries a THREEFOLD increase in cardiovascular mortality compared with matched controls — and this excess risk appears related to GH deficiency itself, since the patients in these studies were already adequately replaced for their OTHER deficient pituitary hormones.
keyPoints: AGHD causes a characteristic body composition shift: less lean mass, more visceral fat, higher waist-to-hip ratio.
Associated with hyperlipidemia, LV dysfunction, hypertension, reduced bone mineral density, and increased fractures.
Adult hypopituitarism carries a threefold increase in cardiovascular mortality, attributable in part to GH deficiency itself (despite replacement of other axes).
items.reference: ADULT GH DEFICIENCY (Presentation and Diagnosis): "Body composition changes are common and include reduced lean body mass, increased fat mass with selective deposition of intraabdominal visceral fat, and increased waist-to-hip ratio. Hyperlipidemia, left ventricular dysfunction, hypertension, and increased plasma fibrinogen levels also may be present... Adult hypopituitarism is associated with a threefold increase in cardiovascular mortality rates in comparison to age- and sex-matched controls, and this may be due to GH deficiency."

items.title: Why the insulin tolerance test is the most validated diagnostic test for adult GH deficiency
items.content:
AGHD is RARE and its clinical features are NONSPECIFIC, so testing must be reserved for patients with predisposing factors (pituitary surgery, pituitary/hypothalamic tumor or granuloma, prior cranial irradiation, radiologic pituitary lesion, or a childhood history of GH replacement).
The INSULIN TOLERANCE TEST is the most validated way to confirm or exclude AGHD:
• Insulin (0.05-0.1 U/kg) lowers glucose to ~40 mg/dL, producing neuroglycopenic symptoms — this hypoglycemic stress is a potent GH secretagogue.
• PEAK GH occurs at ~60 minutes and remains elevated for up to 2 hours.
• ~90% of HEALTHY adults achieve GH >5 µg/L; AGHD is DEFINED by a peak GH response <3 µg/L.
• The test is CONTRAINDICATED in diabetes, ischemic heart disease, cerebrovascular disease, epilepsy, and in elderly patients — because of the deliberate hypoglycemia. ALTERNATIVES (IV arginine, GHRH, oral ghrelin-receptor agonist, glucagon — sometimes combined) are used when insulin tolerance testing is unsafe.
keyPoints: Insulin tolerance test (hypoglycemia to ~40 mg/dL) is the gold-standard GH stimulus, with peak GH at ~60 min.
Normal: GH >5 µg/L in ~90% of healthy adults; AGHD = peak GH <3 µg/L.
Contraindicated in diabetes, ischemic heart disease, cerebrovascular disease, epilepsy, and the elderly — alternatives include arginine, GHRH, ghrelin-receptor agonist, or glucagon.
items.reference: ADULT GH DEFICIENCY (Laboratory Investigation): "The most validated test to distinguish pituitary-sufficient patients from those with AGHD is insulin-induced (0.05–0.1 U/kg) hypoglycemia... peak GH release occurs at 60 min and remains elevated for up to 2 h. About 90% of healthy adults exhibit GH responses >5 µg/L; AGHD is defined by a peak GH response to hypoglycemia of <3 µg/L."

items.title: Why IGF-1 levels alone cannot reliably diagnose adult GH deficiency
items.content:
It is tempting to use IGF-1 — a stable, integrated marker of GH action — as a simple screening test for AGHD, the way it is used to SCREEN for acromegaly. But the logic does not transfer cleanly to the deficiency state:
• Roughly 25% of TRULY GH-deficient adults have LOW-NORMAL (not frankly low) IGF-1 levels — there is substantial OVERLAP with the normal range.
• Therefore, a normal IGF-1 does NOT exclude AGHD, and IGF-1 is NOT sufficiently precise for DIAGNOSIS.
• Its real utility is in MONITORING THERAPEUTIC RESPONSE once GH replacement is started — IGF-1 normalization tracks treatment adequacy — rather than in establishing the initial diagnosis, which requires dynamic (provocative) testing such as the insulin tolerance test.
keyPoints: ~25% of confirmed AGHD patients have low-normal (not clearly low) IGF-1, creating diagnostic overlap with normal individuals.
A normal IGF-1 does NOT exclude AGHD — diagnosis requires provocative testing.
IGF-1's main practical role is monitoring the response to GH replacement, not initial diagnosis.
items.reference: ADULT GH DEFICIENCY (Laboratory Investigation): "A significant proportion (~25%) of truly GH-deficient adults have low-normal IGF-1 levels. Thus, as in the evaluation of GH deficiency in children, valid age-matched IGF-1 measurements provide a useful index of therapeutic responses but are not sufficiently precise for diagnostic purposes."

items.title: GH replacement in adults: dosing targets, sex/age adjustments, and side effects
items.content:
GH replacement in confirmed AGHD is TITRATED rather than given at a fixed dose:
• Starting dose 0.1-0.2 mg/d, titrated up to a maximum of 1.25 mg/d, targeting IGF-1 in the MID-NORMAL range for age- and sex-matched controls.
• WOMEN require HIGHER doses than men (estrogen induces hepatic GH resistance); ELDERLY patients require LESS GH.
• CONTRAINDICATIONS: active neoplasm, intracranial hypertension, uncontrolled diabetes/retinopathy.
• Long-term benefits: sustained normal IGF-1, increased lean mass/decreased fat, increased HDL (without major change in total cholesterol/insulin), and a SLOW (>1 year) increase in lumbar spine bone mineral density; quality-of-life often improves.
• SIDE EFFECTS are mostly dose-related and reversible: ~30% develop fluid retention, joint pain, and carpal tunnel syndrome; up to 40% develop myalgias/paresthesias. Patients with diabetes need careful insulin-dose monitoring, since GH is a counterregulatory hormone and can transiently worsen insulin resistance (though glycemic control often improves over time as visceral fat decreases).
keyPoints: Dose titrated from 0.1-0.2 mg/d up to 1.25 mg/d, targeting mid-normal age/sex-matched IGF-1 — not a fixed dose.
Women need higher doses (estrogen-induced hepatic GH resistance); elderly patients need lower doses.
Common reversible side effects: fluid retention, joint pain, carpal tunnel syndrome (~30%), myalgia/paresthesia (~40%); monitor glycemic control in diabetics.
items.reference: ADULT GH DEFICIENCY (Treatment): "The starting adult dose of 0.1–0.2 mg/d should be titrated (up to a maximum of 1.25 mg/d) to maintain IGF-1 levels in the mid-normal range for age- and sex-matched controls... Women require higher doses than men, and elderly patients require less GH... About 30% of patients exhibit reversible dose-related fluid retention, joint pain, and carpal tunnel syndrome, and up to 40% exhibit myalgias and paresthesia."

items.title: Why secondary adrenal insufficiency lacks the hyperpigmentation and mineralocorticoid deficiency seen in primary adrenal failure
items.content:
Secondary adrenal insufficiency (from pituitary ACTH deficiency) shares some features with primary adrenal failure — fatigue, weakness, anorexia, nausea, vomiting, and occasional hypoglycemia — but TWO classic primary-AI findings are characteristically ABSENT:
• NO HYPERPIGMENTATION: hyperpigmentation in primary AI results from markedly elevated ACTH/POMC-derived peptides (which stimulate melanocytes) as the pituitary tries to overcome adrenal failure. In SECONDARY AI, ACTH is LOW (the source of the problem), so there is no excess melanocyte-stimulating signal.
• NO MINERALOCORTICOID DEFICIENCY: aldosterone secretion is controlled by the renin-angiotensin system, which is INDEPENDENT of ACTH/pituitary function. A pituitary-level lesion therefore spares mineralocorticoid production.
Clinically, these two negatives help distinguish a pituitary/hypothalamic cause of adrenal insufficiency from primary adrenal (Addison's) disease at the bedside, before biochemical confirmation.
keyPoints: Secondary AI shares fatigue/GI symptoms with primary AI but lacks hyperpigmentation (ACTH is low, not high) and lacks mineralocorticoid deficiency (aldosterone is RAA-driven, ACTH-independent).
These two negative findings are useful bedside clues pointing to a pituitary rather than adrenal cause.
Mechanistic link to [[h2-079]] adrenal physiology: aldosterone regulation is preserved in pituitary disease.
items.reference: ACTH DEFICIENCY (Presentation and Diagnosis): "Secondary adrenal insufficiency occurs as a result of pituitary ACTH deficiency. It is characterized by fatigue, weakness, anorexia, nausea, vomiting, and, occasionally, hypoglycemia. In contrast to primary adrenal failure, hypocortisolism associated with pituitary failure usually is not accompanied by hyperpigmentation or mineralocorticoid deficiency."

items.title: ACTH deficiency: laboratory diagnosis and principles of glucocorticoid replacement
items.content:
DIAGNOSIS: 'inappropriately low ACTH in the setting of low cortisol' is the hallmark of diminished ACTH reserve — analogous to the trophic-hormone/target-hormone logic used for the other axes. Low basal cortisol is accompanied by blunted cortisol responses to ACTH stimulation, insulin-induced hypoglycemia, metyrapone, or CRH testing.
CAUSES include: glucocorticoid withdrawal after treatment-induced HPA suppression (most common); isolated ACTH deficiency after successful resection of an ACTH-secreting adenoma (a marker of surgical cure); mass effects of other sellar lesions (usually with other deficits); unmasking during acute illness; and rare TPIT or POMC mutations causing primary ACTH deficiency.
TREATMENT: hydrocortisone replacement, total daily dose ≤20 mg, divided into 2-3 doses; prednisone (5 mg each morning) is an alternative — longer-acting with fewer mineralocorticoid effects. Some clinicians prefer lower maintenance doses to avoid cushingoid side effects. Doses must be increased SEVERALFOLD during acute illness/stress, and patients should wear medical-alert identification.
keyPoints: Diagnosis: inappropriately low ACTH with low cortisol, plus blunted responses to ACTH/insulin-hypoglycemia/metyrapone/CRH testing.
Most common cause is glucocorticoid withdrawal after HPA-axis suppression; isolated post-surgical ACTH deficiency can indicate cure of an ACTH-secreting adenoma.
Hydrocortisone ≤20 mg/d (divided) or prednisone 5 mg each morning, with stress-dose increases during illness.
items.reference: ACTH DEFICIENCY (Laboratory Diagnosis / Treatment): "Inappropriately low ACTH levels in the setting of low cortisol levels are characteristic of diminished ACTH reserve... The total daily dose of hydrocortisone replacement preferably should not exceed 20 mg daily, divided into two or three doses. Prednisone (5 mg each morning) is longer acting and has fewer mineralocorticoid effects than hydrocortisone... Doses should be increased severalfold during periods of acute illness or stress."

items.title: Gonadotropin deficiency: the most common presenting feature of adult hypopituitarism, and its treatment
items.content:
Hypogonadism is the MOST COMMON presenting feature of adult hypopituitarism — even when other axes are also affected — and often signals a hypothalamic or pituitary lesion impairing GnRH production or its delivery through the stalk (e.g., stalk compression, as seen with sellar masses).
TREATMENT depends on sex and whether fertility is desired:
• MEN (no fertility goal): testosterone replacement (IM injections every 1-4 weeks, patches, or gels) maintains secondary sexual characteristics, libido, muscle, and bone mass.
• MEN (fertility desired): gonadotropin injections (hCG or hMG) over 12-18 months, OR pulsatile subcutaneous GnRH (25-150 ng/kg every 2 h via infusion pump) for HYPOTHALAMIC causes.
• WOMEN (no fertility goal): cyclical estrogen + progesterone maintains secondary sexual characteristics, genitourinary mucosal integrity, and bone health.
• WOMEN (fertility desired): gonadotropin therapy for ovulation induction — hMG/recombinant FSH for follicular growth/maturation, then hCG/LH to trigger ovulation; pulsatile GnRH for hypothalamic causes.
keyPoints: Hypogonadism is the most common presenting feature of adult hypopituitarism, often the first sign of a hypothalamic/pituitary lesion.
Non-fertility goals: testosterone (men) or cyclical estrogen/progesterone (women).
Fertility goals: gonadotropins (hCG/hMG, or recombinant FSH + hCG) for either sex; pulsatile GnRH specifically for hypothalamic causes.
items.reference: GONADOTROPIN DEFICIENCY / TREATMENT (Gonadotropin Deficiency): "Hypogonadism is the most common presenting feature of adult hypopituitarism even when other pituitary hormones are also deficient. It is often a harbinger of hypothalamic or pituitary lesions that impair GnRH production or delivery through the pituitary stalk."

items.title: Why insulin-induced hypoglycemia testing of pituitary reserve carries specific contraindications
items.content:
Provocative testing of the HPA axis (and GH axis) often relies on insulin-induced hypoglycemia, because it is a potent physiologic stimulus for both ACTH/cortisol and GH release. ACTH reserve is, in fact, MOST RELIABLY assessed by measuring ACTH and cortisol during this test.
But deliberately inducing hypoglycemia in a patient with SUSPECTED adrenal insufficiency is inherently risky:
• Cortisol is itself a counterregulatory hormone — a patient who cannot mount a cortisol response is also LESS ABLE to recover from the induced hypoglycemia, and is more prone to HYPOTENSION.
• The test is therefore performed CAUTIOUSLY (with monitoring and glucose available) in such patients.
• It is CONTRAINDICATED outright in patients with active CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE (hypoglycemia-induced catecholamine surge can precipitate ischemia) or known SEIZURE DISORDERS (hypoglycemia can trigger seizures).
This is the same safety logic underlying the contraindications for the insulin tolerance test used to diagnose AGHD.
keyPoints: ACTH reserve is most reliably tested via ACTH/cortisol response to insulin-induced hypoglycemia.
Patients with suspected adrenal insufficiency are at higher risk during this test (impaired recovery from hypoglycemia, hypotension) — perform cautiously.
Contraindicated in active coronary artery disease and known seizure disorders.
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (Hypopituitarism): "ACTH reserve is most reliably assessed by measuring ACTH and cortisol levels during insulin-induced hypoglycemia. However, this test should be performed cautiously in patients with suspected adrenal insufficiency because of enhanced susceptibility to hypoglycemia and hypotension. Administering insulin to induce hypoglycemia is contraindicated in patients with active coronary artery disease or known seizure disorders."

items.question: A 50-year-old man with a large nonfunctioning pituitary macroadenoma is found to have low testosterone with low LH/FSH. Following the typical sequential pattern of pituitary hormone loss, which deficiency is most likely to develop next if the tumor continues to compress the gland?
items.explanation: The typical order of trophic hormone loss with progressive pituitary compression is GH > FSH/LH > TSH > ACTH. Since gonadotropin deficiency is already present, TSH deficiency is the next expected step, with ACTH deficiency occurring latest.
items.reference: TABLE 391-1 Etiology of Hypopituitarism: "Trophic hormone failure associated with pituitary compression or destruction usually occurs sequentially: growth hormone > follicle-stimulating hormone > luteinizing hormone > thyroid-stimulating hormone > adrenocorticotropic hormone."

items.question: A patient with a pituitary mass has a free T4 of 0.6 ng/dL (low) and a TSH of 1.8 mU/L (within the reference range). How should this combination be interpreted?
options: Normal thyroid function, since the TSH is within the normal range
Primary hypothyroidism requiring high-dose levothyroxine and TSH monitoring
Secondary (central) hypothyroidism — the TSH should be elevated in response to the low T4 but is inappropriately normal
items.explanation: In primary hypothyroidism, a low T4 should provoke a markedly elevated TSH. Here, the TSH is inappropriately normal given the low T4 — the trophic hormone has failed to rise — indicating secondary (pituitary) hypothyroidism.
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION: "Biochemical diagnosis of pituitary insufficiency is made by demonstrating low levels of respective pituitary trophic hormones in the setting of low levels of target organ hormones. For example, low free thyroxine in the setting of a low or inappropriately normal TSH level suggests secondary hypothyroidism."

items.question: A child has growth retardation and is found to have combined GH, PRL, TSH, and gonadotropin deficiencies. MRI shows an enlarged (not hypoplastic) pituitary gland. Which mutation is most consistent with this presentation?
items.explanation: PROP1 mutations cause a broader combined deficiency (GH, PRL, TSH, and gonadotropins) than isolated Pit-1 mutations (GH, PRL, TSH only), and the pituitary can paradoxically appear enlarged on MRI rather than hypoplastic.
items.reference: DEVELOPMENTAL CAUSES OF HYPOPITUITARISM: "Familial and sporadic PROP1 mutations result in combined GH, PRL, TSH, and gonadotropin deficiency. Over 80% of these patients have growth retardation; by adulthood, all are deficient in TSH and gonadotropins, and a small minority later develop adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) deficiency. In some cases, the pituitary gland appears enlarged on MRI."

items.question: An 18-year-old man presents with absent pubertal development, micropenis, and an inability to smell coffee or perfume. Laboratory testing shows low testosterone with low LH and FSH. What is the most likely diagnosis?
items.explanation: The combination of hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (low testosterone with low LH/FSH) and anosmia/hyposmia is characteristic of Kallmann syndrome, reflecting a shared developmental defect in GnRH neuron and olfactory bulb migration from the olfactory placode.
items.reference: HYPOTHALAMIC ENDOCRINE DYSFUNCTION (Kallmann Syndrome): "Kallmann syndrome results from defective hypothalamic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) synthesis and is associated with anosmia or hyposmia due to olfactory bulb agenesis or hypoplasia."

items.question: A young man with delayed puberty and anosmia undergoes intravenous GnRH stimulation testing, which produces a normal approximately threefold rise in LH within 30 minutes. What does this finding suggest about the site of his defect?
options: A hypothalamic defect with intact pituitary gonadotrope function, consistent with Kallmann syndrome
The result excludes any cause of hypogonadism
items.explanation: A normal gonadotropin rise after exogenous GnRH indicates that the pituitary gonadotropes are intact and responsive — the deficiency must lie upstream, at the hypothalamic level (insufficient endogenous GnRH), as in Kallmann syndrome.
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (Gonadotropin Deficiency): "In the setting of gonadotropin deficiency, a normal gonadotropin response to GnRH indicates intact pituitary gonadotrope function and suggests a hypothalamic abnormality."

items.question: A 10-year-old with obesity, intellectual disability, polydactyly, and progressive night blindness is found to have hypogonadotropic hypogonadism. Which diagnosis best fits this presentation?
items.explanation: Bardet-Biedl syndrome is a genetically heterogeneous ciliopathy characterized by intellectual disability, renal abnormalities, obesity, polydactyly/syndactyly, progressive retinal degeneration (leading to blindness by age 30), and GnRH deficiency in most affected males and about half of affected females.
items.reference: HYPOTHALAMIC ENDOCRINE DYSFUNCTION (Bardet-Biedl Syndrome): "This very rare genetically heterogeneous disorder is characterized by intellectual disability, renal abnormalities, obesity, and hexadactyly, brachydactyly, or syndactyly... GnRH deficiency occurs in 75% of males and half of affected females. Retinal degeneration begins in early childhood, and most patients are blind by age 30."

items.question: An infant with severe hypotonia and hyperphagia later develops hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, obesity, and adult-onset diabetes. Genetic testing shows deletion of paternal chromosome 15q. Chronic GnRH administration restores LH/FSH release in this patient. What does this response indicate about the site of the reproductive axis defect?
options: The defect is at the level of the gonads (primary gonadal failure)
The defect is hypothalamic (deficient endogenous GnRH), with intact pituitary gonadotrope function
The defect is an intrinsic pituitary gonadotrope abnormality unresponsive to GnRH
The result cannot localize the defect
items.explanation: Restoration of LH/FSH release with chronic exogenous GnRH indicates that the pituitary gonadotropes are capable of responding when adequately stimulated — the defect lies in deficient endogenous hypothalamic GnRH production, as occurs in Prader-Willi syndrome.
items.reference: HYPOTHALAMIC ENDOCRINE DYSFUNCTION (Prader-Willi Syndrome): "Deficient GnRH synthesis is suggested by the observation that chronic GnRH treatment restores pituitary LH and FSH release."

items.question: A woman who delivered 6 weeks ago presents with headache. MRI shows a pituitary mass resembling an adenoma, PRL is mildly elevated, and ESR is elevated. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Consider lymphocytic hypophysitis and a trial of glucocorticoid therapy before pursuing surgery
Start cabergoline for a presumed prolactinoma without further evaluation
items.explanation: A postpartum woman with a new pituitary mass, mild hyperprolactinemia, and an elevated ESR should be evaluated for lymphocytic hypophysitis, which can mimic an adenoma on MRI. Glucocorticoid treatment often leads to resolution over months, potentially avoiding unnecessary surgery.
items.reference: ACQUIRED HYPOPITUITARISM (Lymphocytic Hypophysitis): "Because it may be indistinguishable from a pituitary adenoma on MRI, hypophysitis should be considered in a postpartum woman with a newly diagnosed pituitary mass before an unnecessary surgical intervention is undertaken. The inflammatory process often resolves after several months of glucocorticoid treatment."

items.question: A patient receiving ipilimumab for metastatic melanoma develops fatigue and hypotension and is found to have secondary adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Start pituitary hormone replacement (with or without high-dose glucocorticoids); immunotherapy can usually be continued
Observation only, since the findings will resolve spontaneously without treatment
items.explanation: CTLA-4 inhibitors such as ipilimumab cause hypophysitis with variable hormone deficiencies in up to 20% of patients. Pituitary hormone replacement, with or without high-dose glucocorticoids, can be safely given while continuing immunotherapy.
items.reference: ACQUIRED HYPOPITUITARISM (Immunotherapy and Hypophysitis): "Pituitary hormone replacement, with or without high-dose glucocorticoids, may be safely tolerated with continued immunotherapy."

items.question: A woman experiences severe postpartum hemorrhage and subsequently fails to lactate, develops amenorrhea, and reports persistent fatigue and hypotension. What is the most likely diagnosis and underlying mechanism?
options: Sheehan's syndrome — postpartum pituitary infarction occurring in a gland enlarged by pregnancy-related hyperplasia
items.explanation: Pregnancy causes physiologic pituitary hyperplasia, increasing vulnerability to hemorrhage/infarction during severe peripartum hemorrhage/hypotension — resulting in Sheehan's syndrome, presenting with failure of lactation, amenorrhea, and other features of hypopituitarism.
items.reference: ACQUIRED HYPOPITUITARISM (Pituitary Apoplexy): "Pituitary apoplexy may occur spontaneously in a preexisting pituitary adenoma; postpartum (Sheehan's syndrome)... The hyperplastic enlargement of the pituitary, which occurs normally during pregnancy, increases the risk for hemorrhage and infarction."

items.question: A patient with a known pituitary macroadenoma develops sudden severe headache, diplopia from a third-nerve palsy, and progressive visual field loss. What is the most appropriate management?
options: High-dose glucocorticoids and observation only, with no surgical intervention
Immediate initiation of a dopamine agonist
items.explanation: Significant or progressive visual loss, cranial nerve palsy, or loss of consciousness in pituitary apoplexy are indications for urgent surgical decompression, as visual recovery is inversely related to the time elapsed before surgery.
items.reference: ACQUIRED HYPOPITUITARISM (Pituitary Apoplexy): "Those with significant or progressive visual loss, cranial nerve palsy, or loss of consciousness require urgent surgical decompression. Visual recovery after sellar surgery is inversely correlated with the length of time after the acute event."

items.question: An incidental MRI finding of an empty sella is noted in an asymptomatic patient with normal pituitary function tests. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: No specific treatment, but periodic monitoring because hypopituitarism may develop insidiously
Lifelong high-dose glucocorticoid replacement starting now
items.explanation: Most patients with an empty sella have normal pituitary function because the residual rim of pituitary tissue remains functional, but hypopituitarism can develop insidiously over time, so periodic monitoring is appropriate rather than immediate intervention.
items.reference: ACQUIRED HYPOPITUITARISM (Empty Sella): "These patients usually have normal pituitary function, implying that the surrounding rim of pituitary tissue is fully functional. Hypopituitarism, however, may develop insidiously."

items.question: A patient who received 54 Gy of cranial irradiation for a brain tumor 8 years ago has normal anterior pituitary function on testing performed shortly after treatment. What is the most appropriate long-term plan?
options: No further testing is needed since initial post-treatment results were normal
Periodic, long-term reassessment of anterior pituitary function, since deficits typically develop over 5-15 years
Annual MRI only, without any hormone testing
Start empirical GH replacement now regardless of testing results
items.explanation: Hypopituitarism after cranial irradiation reflects gradual hypothalamic injury and develops over 5-15 years, so long-term periodic endocrine reassessment is needed even if initial post-treatment testing is normal.
items.reference: ACQUIRED HYPOPITUITARISM (Cranial Irradiation): "The development of hypopituitarism occurs over 5–15 years and usually reflects hypothalamic damage rather than primary destruction of pituitary cells."

items.question: A short child for age has a random serum GH level of 0.5 µg/L. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Diagnose GH deficiency based on this single low value
Perform provocative GH stimulation testing, since random GH levels cannot distinguish normal from deficient secretion
Start recombinant GH replacement immediately without further testing
Measure only a second random GH level at a different time of day
items.explanation: Because GH is secreted in pulses, a single random GH level (even if low) does not distinguish normal children from those with true GH deficiency. Provocative stimulation testing (target GH >7 µg/L) is required.
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (Disorders of Growth and Development): "Random GH measurements do not distinguish children with normal GH levels from those with true GH deficiency."

items.subtopic: Growth Disorders — GH Deficiency in Children
items.question: A neonate presents with hypoglycemia, micropenis, and a high-pitched voice. Isolated GH deficiency is suspected. What is the mechanism underlying the hypoglycemia in this condition?
options: Excess insulin secretion from pancreatic beta-cell hyperplasia
Relatively unopposed insulin action due to the absence of GH's normal counter-regulatory effect
items.explanation: GH normally opposes insulin's effects on glucose metabolism. In its absence, insulin action is relatively unopposed, predisposing to hypoglycemia — a classic feature of isolated GH deficiency in infants alongside micropenis and a high-pitched voice.
items.reference: DISORDERS OF GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT (GH Deficiency in Children): "Isolated GH deficiency is characterized by short stature, micropenis, increased fat, high-pitched voice, and a propensity to hypoglycemia due to relatively unopposed insulin action."

items.question: A child with severe proportionate dwarfism has low basal GH levels that fail to rise after GHRH, GH-releasing peptide, OR insulin-induced hypoglycemia, with anterior pituitary hypoplasia on MRI. Which defect best explains this pattern?
items.explanation: Failure of GH to rise with GHRH, GH-releasing peptide, AND insulin-induced hypoglycemia together with anterior pituitary hypoplasia points to a defect in the GHRH receptor itself, which is required for somatotrope proliferation and responsiveness to all of these stimuli.
items.reference: DISORDERS OF GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT (GHRH Receptor Mutations): "Recessive mutations of the GHRH receptor gene in subjects with severe proportionate dwarfism are associated with low basal GH levels that cannot be stimulated by exogenous GHRH, GH-releasing peptide, or insulin-induced hypoglycemia, as well as anterior pituitary hypoplasia."

items.question: A child with severe short stature has GH levels that are normal-to-high, decreased GH-binding protein (GHBP), and low IGF-1. What is the most likely diagnosis and mechanism?
options: GH deficiency due to pituitary hypoplasia
Laron syndrome — a GH receptor mutation causing GH insensitivity
items.explanation: GH receptor mutations cause GH insensitivity (Laron syndrome): GH secretion is normal or even elevated (since IGF-1 negative feedback is impaired), but circulating GHBP (derived from the receptor) is low and IGF-1 generation is impaired, producing growth failure.
items.reference: DISORDERS OF GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT (GH Insensitivity): "Homozygous or heterozygous mutations of the GH receptor are associated with partial or complete GH insensitivity and growth failure (Laron syndrome). The diagnosis is based on normal or high GH levels, with decreased circulating GH-binding protein (GHBP), and low IGF-1 levels."

items.question: A child with short stature and normal-to-elevated GH but low IGF-1 also has a history of recurrent severe infections and autoimmune disease. Which mutation best unifies this presentation?
items.explanation: STAT5B is required both for downstream GH receptor signaling (its loss reproduces the GH-insensitivity biochemical pattern of high GH/low IGF-1) and for cytokine signaling in immune cells, so STAT5B mutations uniquely combine growth failure with immunodeficiency.
items.reference: DISORDERS OF GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT (GH Insensitivity): "STAT5B mutations result in both immunodeficiency as well as abrogated GH signaling, leading to short stature with normal or elevated GH levels and low IGF-1 levels."

items.subtopic: Growth Disorders — Nutritional and Psychosocial Short Stature
items.question: A child from a neglectful home environment has acquired short stature with normal-to-elevated GH levels, low IGF-1, delayed speech, and excessive eating when away from home. A trial of GH produces an attenuated growth response. What is the most effective intervention?
options: High-dose recombinant GH therapy regardless of environment
Placement in a nurturing environment, which restores normal growth rates
items.explanation: Psychosocial short stature is caused by emotional/social deprivation and is characterized by attenuated GH responsiveness; the underlying biochemical pattern (high-normal GH, low IGF-1) resembles GH insensitivity, but the defining feature is that a nurturing environment — not GH or IGF-1 therapy — restores growth.
items.reference: DISORDERS OF GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT (Psychosocial Short Stature): "Emotional and social deprivation lead to growth retardation accompanied by delayed speech, discordant hyperphagia, and an attenuated response to administered GH. A nurturing environment restores growth rates."

items.question: An adult with hypopituitarism and untreated GH deficiency has increased visceral fat, reduced lean body mass, hyperlipidemia, and left ventricular dysfunction. What additional long-term risk is most associated with this presentation?
options: Decreased overall cardiovascular mortality compared to controls
A threefold increase in cardiovascular mortality compared with age- and sex-matched controls
items.explanation: Adult hypopituitarism with untreated GH deficiency is associated with adverse body composition and metabolic changes and carries a threefold increase in cardiovascular mortality compared to matched controls.
items.reference: ADULT GH DEFICIENCY (Presentation and Diagnosis): "Adult hypopituitarism is associated with a threefold increase in cardiovascular mortality rates in comparison to age- and sex-matched controls, and this may be due to GH deficiency."

items.question: An adult with hypopituitarism undergoes an insulin tolerance test for suspected adult GH deficiency. At 60 minutes (the time of peak GH response), the GH level is 2 µg/L. How should this result be interpreted?
options: Normal GH reserve, since >90% of healthy adults exceed 5 µg/L and 2 µg/L falls within normal limits
Consistent with adult GH deficiency, which is defined by a peak GH response to hypoglycemia of <3 µg/L
The test is invalid and must be repeated using a higher insulin dose
items.explanation: The insulin tolerance test is the most validated test for adult GH deficiency; about 90% of healthy adults exceed 5 µg/L at peak, while AGHD is defined by a peak GH response to hypoglycemia of less than 3 µg/L — so a peak of 2 µg/L is diagnostic of AGHD.
items.reference: ADULT GH DEFICIENCY (Laboratory Investigation): "About 90% of healthy adults exhibit GH responses >5 µg/L; AGHD is defined by a peak GH response to hypoglycemia of <3 µg/L."

items.question: An adult with suspected GH deficiency has an IGF-1 level in the low-normal range for age. What is the most appropriate interpretation?
options: A normal-range IGF-1 excludes GH deficiency entirely
Low-normal IGF-1 does not exclude GH deficiency — about 25% of truly GH-deficient adults have low-normal IGF-1, so dynamic stimulation testing is needed for diagnosis
IGF-1 is the single most accurate diagnostic test, and no further testing is needed
IGF-1 levels are unaffected by GH status in adults
items.explanation: Roughly a quarter of adults with true GH deficiency have low-normal IGF-1 levels, so IGF-1 alone is insufficiently precise for diagnosis — it is more useful for monitoring therapeutic response than for the initial diagnosis, which requires dynamic testing such as the insulin tolerance test.
items.reference: ADULT GH DEFICIENCY (Laboratory Investigation): "A significant proportion (~25%) of truly GH-deficient adults have low-normal IGF-1 levels. Thus, as in the evaluation of GH deficiency in children, valid age-matched IGF-1 measurements provide a useful index of therapeutic responses but are not sufficiently precise for diagnostic purposes."

items.question: A 60-year-old woman and a 30-year-old man both begin GH replacement for adult GH deficiency. How should their dosing typically differ, and what side effects should be monitored during titration?
options: Women generally require higher GH doses than men, but elderly patients require less GH than younger patients; monitor for fluid retention, joint pain, and carpal tunnel syndrome
Women require lower doses than men because they are more sensitive to GH
Dosing is identical regardless of age or sex, and no side-effect monitoring is required
Both should receive the maximum dose of 1.25 mg/d from the outset
items.explanation: GH replacement is started at 0.1-0.2 mg/d and titrated to a maximum of 1.25 mg/d, targeting mid-normal age- and sex-matched IGF-1. Women require higher doses than men, and elderly patients require less. Reversible fluid retention, joint pain, carpal tunnel syndrome, myalgias, and paresthesia are common dose-related side effects.
items.reference: ADULT GH DEFICIENCY (Treatment): "Women require higher doses than men, and elderly patients require less GH... About 30% of patients exhibit reversible dose-related fluid retention, joint pain, and carpal tunnel syndrome, and up to 40% exhibit myalgias and paresthesia."

items.question: A patient with a pituitary mass presents with fatigue, anorexia, and nausea. Unlike a patient with primary adrenal insufficiency, this patient has no hyperpigmentation and normal serum potassium with no salt-wasting. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Secondary adrenal insufficiency due to pituitary ACTH deficiency
items.explanation: Secondary adrenal insufficiency from ACTH deficiency causes glucocorticoid deficiency (fatigue, anorexia, nausea, vomiting, occasional hypoglycemia) but spares mineralocorticoid production (regulated mainly by the renin-angiotensin system) and does not cause the hyperpigmentation seen in primary adrenal failure (which results from elevated ACTH/POMC peptides).
items.reference: ACTH DEFICIENCY (Presentation and Diagnosis): "In contrast to primary adrenal failure, hypocortisolism associated with pituitary failure usually is not accompanied by hyperpigmentation or mineralocorticoid deficiency."

items.question: A patient with documented ACTH deficiency is started on hydrocortisone replacement. What is an appropriate maintenance regimen, and how should dosing be adjusted during acute illness?
options: A total daily dose not exceeding about 20 mg, divided into two or three doses, with several-fold dose increases during acute illness or stress
A single fixed dose of 100 mg daily regardless of clinical status
Mandatory addition of mineralocorticoid replacement in all patients
No dose adjustment is ever needed regardless of illness severity
items.explanation: Standard glucocorticoid replacement for ACTH deficiency is hydrocortisone at no more than ~20 mg/day in divided doses (or prednisone 5 mg each morning), with the critical caveat that doses must be increased several-fold during acute illness or stress to prevent adrenal crisis.
items.reference: ACTH DEFICIENCY (Treatment): "The total daily dose of hydrocortisone replacement preferably should not exceed 20 mg daily, divided into two or three doses... Doses should be increased severalfold during periods of acute illness or stress."

items.question: A man with hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, currently maintained on testosterone gel, now desires fertility. What change in management is most appropriate to restore spermatogenesis?
options: Continue testosterone replacement alone, as it will eventually restore spermatogenesis
Switch to gonadotropin injections (hCG with or without hMG) over 12-18 months, or pulsatile GnRH therapy via subcutaneous infusion pump
Add GH replacement, the primary driver of spermatogenesis
items.explanation: Exogenous testosterone suppresses gonadotropins further and does not restore fertility. To induce spermatogenesis in hypogonadotropic men, gonadotropin therapy (hCG ± hMG) over 12-18 months or pulsatile GnRH therapy is required.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Gonadotropin Deficiency): "Gonadotropin injections (hCG or human menopausal gonadotropin [hMG]) over 12–18 months are used to restore fertility. Pulsatile GnRH therapy (25–150 ng/kg every 2 h), administered by a subcutaneous infusion pump, is also effective for treatment of hypothalamic hypogonadism when fertility is desired."

items.question: A premenopausal woman with hypothalamic amenorrhea due to gonadotropin deficiency desires pregnancy. Which approach is used to induce ovulation?
options: Gonadotropin therapy (hMG or recombinant FSH for follicular development, followed by hCG or LH to trigger ovulation), or pulsatile GnRH therapy for hypothalamic causes
items.explanation: Cyclical estrogen-progesterone maintains secondary sexual characteristics and prevents osteoporosis but does not induce ovulation. Fertility requires gonadotropin therapy (hMG/recombinant FSH for follicular maturation, hCG/LH to trigger ovulation) or, for hypothalamic causes, pulsatile GnRH therapy.
items.reference: TREATMENT (Gonadotropin Deficiency, women): "Gonadotropin therapy is used for ovulation induction. Follicular growth and maturation are initiated using hMG or recombinant FSH; hCG or human luteinizing hormone is subsequently injected to induce ovulation. As in men, pulsatile GnRH therapy can be used to treat hypothalamic causes of gonadotropin deficiency."

items.question: A patient with suspected hypopituitarism and a history of active coronary artery disease is being considered for assessment of ACTH reserve via insulin-induced hypoglycemia. What is the most appropriate course of action?
options: Proceed with the insulin tolerance test as planned, since it is the most validated test of ACTH reserve
Avoid insulin-induced hypoglycemia testing, since it is contraindicated in patients with active coronary artery disease or known seizure disorders due to the risk of hypoglycemia and hypotension
Increase the insulin dose to ensure an adequate hypoglycemic stimulus
Perform the test only if the patient is also diabetic
items.explanation: Although insulin-induced hypoglycemia is the most reliable test of ACTH reserve, it must be avoided in patients with active coronary artery disease or seizure disorders because of the risks of hypoglycemia and hypotension in these populations.
items.reference: LABORATORY INVESTIGATION (Hypopituitarism): "Administering insulin to induce hypoglycemia is contraindicated in patients with active coronary artery disease or known seizure disorders."

items.statement: PROP1 mutations cause combined GH, PRL, TSH, and gonadotropin deficiency, and a small minority of patients later develop ACTH deficiency as well.
items.explanation: PROP1 mutations cause the broadest combined pituitary hormone deficiency among the transcription factor defects, encompassing GH, PRL, TSH, and gonadotropins, with late ACTH deficiency in a minority.
items.reference: DEVELOPMENTAL CAUSES OF HYPOPITUITARISM: "Familial and sporadic PROP1 mutations result in combined GH, PRL, TSH, and gonadotropin deficiency... a small minority later develop adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) deficiency."

items.statement: In Bardet-Biedl syndrome, GnRH deficiency occurs in approximately 75% of affected males but in only about half of affected females.
items.explanation: Bardet-Biedl syndrome shows sex-dependent penetrance of GnRH deficiency, affecting roughly three-quarters of males but only about half of females.
items.reference: HYPOTHALAMIC ENDOCRINE DYSFUNCTION (Bardet-Biedl Syndrome): "GnRH deficiency occurs in 75% of males and half of affected females."

items.statement: Lymphocytic hypophysitis occurs most often in postpartum women and can be indistinguishable from a pituitary adenoma on MRI.
items.explanation: Lymphocytic hypophysitis classically presents postpartum with hyperprolactinemia and a pituitary mass that mimics an adenoma on imaging, and it often improves with glucocorticoid therapy.
items.reference: ACQUIRED HYPOPITUITARISM (Lymphocytic Hypophysitis): "This occurs most often in postpartum women... Because it may be indistinguishable from a pituitary adenoma on MRI, hypophysitis should be considered in a postpartum woman with a newly diagnosed pituitary mass before an unnecessary surgical intervention is undertaken."

items.statement: Hypophysitis caused by PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors such as pembrolizumab and nivolumab always presents immediately after the first dose, unlike CTLA-4 inhibitor-associated hypophysitis.
items.explanation: PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor-associated hypophysitis may show a delayed presentation, in contrast to the often earlier presentation seen with CTLA-4 inhibitors such as ipilimumab.
items.reference: ACQUIRED HYPOPITUITARISM (Immunotherapy and Hypophysitis): "Hypophysitis is also reported with PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors (e.g., pembrolizumab and nivolumab) and may show delayed presentation."

items.statement: An incidentally discovered empty sella on MRI always indicates significant hypopituitarism requiring immediate hormone replacement.
items.explanation: Most patients with an empty sella have normal pituitary function because the surrounding rim of pituitary tissue remains functional, although hypopituitarism may develop insidiously over time.
items.reference: ACQUIRED HYPOPITUITARISM (Empty Sella): "These patients usually have normal pituitary function, implying that the surrounding rim of pituitary tissue is fully functional."

items.statement: After cranial irradiation, ACTH deficiency is typically the FIRST hormone deficiency to appear, while GH deficiency develops last.
items.explanation: The opposite is true: GH deficiency is the most common and earliest deficiency after cranial irradiation, followed by gonadotropin, TSH, and finally ACTH deficiency.
